critique.summary: The article expresses gratitude to Clockwise users and highlights the impact of the service in improving calendar management by creating 8 million hours of focus time and rescheduling 23 million meetings. It implies that Clockwise has been acquired by Salesforce and will be shutting down next week.
mainPoints: Clockwise has been acquired by Salesforce.
Clockwise will be shutting down next week.
40,000 organizations used Clockwise to improve calendar management.
Clockwise created 8 million hours of focus time.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about Clockwise's impact (hours of focus time created and meetings rescheduled) are plausible and consistent with the company's stated mission. However, there is no direct confirmation from the article or external sources that Clockwise has been acquired by Salesforce or that it is shutting down next week. The official Clockwise website and recent news do not currently confirm such an acquisition or shutdown, making these claims unverified and potentially misleading.
considerations: No official announcement or press release from Salesforce or Clockwise confirming the acquisition or shutdown was cited.
The article lacks multiple sources or perspectives to verify the claims.
The impact metrics provided (focus time and meetings rescheduled) are not independently verifiable from the article alone.
Users should verify the status of Clockwise through official company communications or trusted news outlets.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the acquisition of Clockwise by Salesforce and the implications of its shutdown. Commenters express a mix of concern for employees, skepticism about Salesforce's strategy, and reflections on the nature of the product.

comments.author: singron
comments.text:
I used to work at Clockwise and am (was?) a common shareholder. I'm happy that many employees got to get a job at Salesforce. I'm sure it was tough to swallow some pride and recommend Reclaim, who was our strongest competitor in the space (or at least the one we talked about the most). Reclaim was acquired by Dropbox a while ago, although Dropbox wanted them to continue to run and develop the product there.

I also applaud them for not selling the data (as promised in the ToS). There was always a strong commitment to that from day 1, but I'm glad to see that wasn't an option when times got harder. Calendar data sometimes has really sensitive stuff in it, and it would have been a massive betrayal to do anything but delete it after a shutdown.

If you are interested in a more detailed piece about a company struggling in this space, I recommend Rise's shutdown announcement last year. We read this at Clockwise and unfortunately felt it in our bones. There is an ironic Clockwise callout in the piece if you can spot it.

https://www.risecalendar.com/blog/sunsetting-rise

I'm obviously not part of the decision, but I'm sorry the shutoff for users is so soon. Also, please don't revoke your Clockwise app authorization before the shutoff, since that will prevent Clockwise from cleaning up your calendar. If you want to cleanly turn off Clockwise before the shutoff, you can go through the normal deactivation process at https://www.getclockwise.com/uninstall.

It's a huge bummer for me too to have worked on something for years and then to have it suddenly vanish one day.

If you are looking to start a new company in this space, I'll gladly offer my services to talk you out of it. If any die-hard users want to make a self-hosted tool, I'm happy to give some tips from my experience. I know at least one large company has an internal tool like Clockwise's autopilot/flexible meetings.
comments.analysis: This comment provides personal insight and expresses empathy for employees, making it a valuable contribution to the discussion.

comments.author: paxys
comments.text:
Salesforce has lost half its market cap in the last ~year. Spending time and money to acquire a calendar scheduler shows just how badly they have lost the plot.

I know this is an HN meme but can someone look at https://www.getclockwise.com/overview and explain why an internal team couldn't build this in a couple of weeks? And it's not like Salesforce is lacking engineers - they employ 83,000 (!!) people globally.
comments.analysis: The comment critiques Salesforce's acquisition strategy, reflecting a critical perspective on corporate decisions, though it may come off as overly harsh.

comments.author: pmdr
comments.text:
> it has been our mission to help the world make time for what matters. We've built a business to be proud of

There must be a lot of pride and meaning in being run over by Saleforce's money truck.
comments.analysis: This comment uses sarcasm to critique the acquisition, but it lacks constructive elements and may detract from a serious discussion.

comments.author: Animats
comments.text:
Salesforce seems to be doing a shutdown of many of their "pre-AI" products.[1]

[1] https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/controlled-demolition-salesfo...
comments.analysis: This comment offers a relevant observation about Salesforce's strategy, contributing to the understanding of the company's direction.

comments.author: keeganpoppen
comments.text: sorry in advance to their employees as they go up against the value buzzsaw that is Salesforce as an acquirer. i have years of firsthand experience, and there was not a single win in my entire tenure there.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses concern for employees, which adds an emotional layer to the discussion, though it lacks specific details.

comments.author: john_strinlai
comments.text:
seems like a rather short time frame considering they are just deleting the data, links, calendar events, past invoices, etc.?

hopefully no one paying for their service decided to take a 1 week vacation starting tomorrow.
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about the data deletion process, contributing to the discussion with a practical perspective.

comments.author: dinamic
comments.text: Why would a company acquire the other one just to shut it down?
comments.analysis: The question posed is relevant and invites further discussion about corporate strategy, though it lacks depth.

comments.author: anthonySs
comments.text: i wish i had insights into the minds of the MBAs that make decisions like this
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with corporate decision-making, but it does not provide substantial analysis or information.

comments.author: bombcar
comments.text: Interesting tool, but it seems to be more of a feature than a product.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a critical perspective on the product's value, though it lacks elaboration.

comments.author: pmarreck
comments.text: Hope they enjoy working on Java code... Forever... With 3 month release cycles, no CD... LOL
comments.analysis: While humorous, this comment detracts from the serious nature of the discussion and does not contribute constructively.
